---
title: "1.8 Documentation with Comments and Preconditions"
subtitle: "Part 2: Preconditions and Postconditions"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Conditions in API documentation

Many methods in API libraries have <span class="term">preconditions</span> and <span class="term">postconditions</span> described in their comments.

These assumptions are useful to programmers who want to use that method and get the correct results.

## Preconditions

### What the method expects

A <span class="term">precondition</span> (前置条件) is a condition that must be true for your method code to work.

For example, the parameters, the data that you give the method to do its job, may need to have values within limits and not be `null`.

The methods could check for these preconditions, but they do not have to.

The precondition is what the method expects in order to do its job properly.

## Postconditions

### What the method promises

A <span class="term">postcondition</span> (后置条件) is a condition that is true after running the method.

It is what the method promises to do.

Postconditions describe the outcome of running the method, for example what is being returned from the method or the changes to the state.

## Division and Square Root

### Examples of preconditions

One precondition already discussed is that divisors cannot be zero in expressions, because that will cause the code to have a runtime error or exception.

Many math functions have preconditions about their operands because not every mathematical operation is defined for every value.

For example, computing the square root of a negative number is undefined.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: math-preconditions`

The following code uses the square root method in Java, which has a precondition that the number that you give it is not negative.

When you click run, the compiler will not catch the error, but it will return the error value `NaN`.

Can you fix the value of `num` so that it does not return `NaN`?

What is the precondition for the `Math.sqrt` method?

## Broken Code

### `math-preconditions`

```java
public class SqRoot
{
   public static void main(String[] args)
   {
         double num = -4;
         System.out.println(Math.sqrt(num));
   }
}
```

`NaN` stands for "not a number".

## Test Requirements

### `math-preconditions`

Runestone checks that the submitted code still contains:

```java
SqRoot
```

The teaching check is conceptual: change `num` so `Math.sqrt(num)` receives a nonnegative value and does not return `NaN`.

## Turtle `forward` Documentation

### `@param`, precondition, postcondition

Here is an example of preconditions, postconditions, and `@param` in the Turtle code used for animating turtle drawings.

The Turtle `forward` method's precondition is that the amount of `pixels` forward should be between `0` and the width and height of the world.

If it receives a value out of this range, it sets `pixels` to the closest legal values that it can so that the turtle appears just at the edge of the world.

## Turtle `forward` Code {.fit-small}

### Documentation comment

```java
/**
  * Method to move the turtle forward the given number of pixels
  * @param pixels the number of pixels to walk forward in the heading direction
  * Preconditions: parameters pixel is between 0 and
  *    the width and height of the world.
  * Postconditions: the turtle is moved forward by pixels amount
  *   but stays within the width and height of the world.
  */
 public void forward(int pixels)
 {
   /* code to move the turtle forward */
 }
```

## Coding Exercise

### Try to break the precondition

Try to break the preconditions of the `Turtle forward` method below.

Try to make the turtle go completely off screen by changing the number of pixels given to the `forward` method.

What happens if you put in negative numbers?

## Program to Experiment With

### `activecode:: turtle-preconditions`

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class TurtlePreconditions
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World habitat = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle yertle = new Turtle(habitat);

        // Change 100 pixels below to a larger number
        //   to try to go off screen to break preconditions
        yertle.forward(100);

        // code to turn and come back down
        yertle.turnLeft();
        yertle.forward();
        yertle.turnLeft();
        yertle.forward();

        habitat.show(true);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `turtle-preconditions`

Runestone checks that the code has changed from the original value:

```java
forward(100)
```

Expected result: `Change forward(100) to try to break preconditions` should be `true`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define a precondition as what must be true before method code works properly
- define a postcondition as what is true after the method runs
- explain why division by zero and negative square root inputs are precondition examples
- fix `num = -4` so `Math.sqrt(num)` does not return `NaN`
- identify `pixels` as the parameter documented by `@param`
- change `forward(100)` to test the Turtle method's documented precondition
:::

## End

### Continue to use cases

The next deck uses preconditions and postconditions in use-case examples and a group challenge.
